2: tēs philoxenias mē epilanthanese, dia tautēs gar elathon tines xenisontes angelous — "do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels without knowing it" (philoxenias: love-of-stranger, hospitality — philos + xenos: affection toward the foreigner; elathon: escaped notice, were unaware — aorist of lanthanō: to escape notice): the construction elathon... xenisontes (escaped notice while showing hospitality to) is the idiom for doing something without realizing it. The Genesis precedent (Abraham and Lot) establishes that ordinary hospitality can be divine encounter.
7: mnēmoneuete tōn hēgoumenōn hymōn, hoitines elalēsan hymin ton logon tou theou, hōn anatheoroyntes tēn ekbasin tēs anastrophēs, mimeisthe tēn pistin — "remember your leaders, those who spoke to you the word of God. Consider the outcome of their way of life, and imitate their faith" (hēgoumenōn: leaders, those who lead — the present active participle as a title: those who exercise leadership): the specific "outcome" (ekbasis: exit, outcome, result — cf. 1 Corinthians 10:13) of the leaders' lives suggests these are leaders who have died — possibly martyrs whose death-pattern the community is to contemplate and emulate. Mimeisthe tēn pistin: imitate their faith — the faith-quality is the object of imitation, not specific actions.
8: Iēsous Christos echthes kai sēmeron ho autos kai eis tous aiōnas — "Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever" (echthes: yesterday; sēmeron: today; eis tous aiōnas: unto the ages, forever): the temporal statement covers past-present-future in one compact predication. In context (after the call to remember past leaders and before the warning against strange teachings), the unchangeableness of Christ grounds the community's resistance to doctrinal novelty — what was taught by the past leaders under the eternal Christ remains valid under the same eternal Christ. The verse is not a general statement of divine immutability but a contextual anchor against theological drift.
10: echomen thysiastērion ex hou phagein ouk echousin exousian hoi tē skēnē latreuontes — "we have an altar from which those who serve the tent have no right to eat" (thysiastērion: altar — the place of sacrifice; exousian phagein: right/authority to eat): the altar-eating privilege was a genuine feature of the Levitical system: the priests ate portions of certain sacrifices. The community's altar is the cross; the eating is participation in the benefits of Christ's sacrifice (cf. 1 Corinthians 10:18 on the relationship between eating and altar-participation). Those who maintain the tent-system forfeit the right to eat from the Christological altar.
12: dio kai Iēsous, hina hagiasē dia tou idiou haimatos ton laon, exō tēs pylēs epathen — "so Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood" (exō tēs pylēs: outside the gate — the Jerusalem city gate; the crucifixion site outside the city walls is the typological fulfillment of the atonement-animals burned outside the camp): hina hagiasē... ton laon (that he might sanctify the people): the purpose of the outside-the-gate suffering is the sanctification of the people — the same purpose as the burning of the sin-offering outside the camp. The spatial dislocation of the sacrifice is the mechanism of the community's purification.
15: di' autou oun anapherōmen thysian aineseōs dia pantos tō theō, tout' estin karpon cheiloōn homologountōn tō onomati autou — "through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of lips that acknowledge his name" (anapherōmen thysian aineseōs: let us offer up a sacrifice of praise — anapherō: to carry up, to bring to the altar; the same verb used for the Aaronic offerings [Hebrews 5:1] and for Christ bearing sins [Hebrews 9:28]): karpon cheiloōn homologountōn (fruit of lips that confess/acknowledge): Hosea 14:2 LXX uses karpon cheiloōn (fruit of lips) as the replacement for animal sacrifice after judgment — Hebrews inherits this prophetic substitution and grounds it in Christ's completed sacrifice.
20: Ho de theos tēs eirēnēs, ho anagagōn ek nekrōn ton poimena tōn probatōn ton megan en haimati diathēkēs aiōniou, ton kyrion hēmōn Iēsoun — "now may the God of peace who brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, the great shepherd of the sheep, by the blood of the eternal covenant" (anagagōn ek nekrōn: having brought up from the dead — anagō: to bring up/lead up; the unusual verb choice [not the standard egeirō = raise] recalls the Shepherd-from-the-sea imagery of Isaiah 63:11; the bringing-up from death echoes the bringing-up from Egypt): en haimati diathēkēs aiōniou: by/in the blood of the eternal covenant — the resurrection is grounded in the eternal covenant sealed by blood; the blood precedes and grounds the resurrection. Ton poimena ton megan: the great shepherd — the superlative characterization of the risen Christ as shepherd fulfills the OT shepherd-messiah hope (Ezekiel 34:23-24; Hebrews 37:24).
1: Hē philadelphia menetō — "let brotherly love continue" (philadelphia: love of brothers — philos [affection/friendship-love] + adelphos [brother]; the compound covers the warm, familial bond of the community): menetō: let it remain, let it continue — present imperative of menō: to stay, to abide; the call is not to establish what is new but to maintain what already exists. The word choice philadelphia rather than agapē is notable — the sibling-affection word is appropriate for a community whose members are the adelphoi (brothers and sisters) of the Son who is not ashamed to call them such (Hebrews 2:11-12).
3: mimnēskesthe tōn desmion hōs syndedemenoi, tōn kakouchoumenōn hōs kai autoi ontes en sōmati — "remember those who are in prison as if you were their fellow prisoners, and those who are mistreated as if you yourselves were suffering" (hōs syndedemenoi: as if bound-together-with — syndesmoi: co-prisoners; the syn-prefix expresses the imaginative solidarity): hōs kai autoi ontes en sōmati: as yourselves also being in the body — en sōmati: the bodily existence that makes suffering real and shareable; the reminder that the community shares the same physical vulnerability. The identification is not merely empathetic but bodily — the same flesh is the site of both comfort and suffering.
4: Timios ho gamos en pasin kai hē koitē amiantos; pornous gar kai moichous krinei ho theos — "let marriage be held in honor by all, and let the marriage bed be kept pure, for God will judge the sexually immoral and the adulterous" (timios: honored, precious — the same word for "precious" in 1 Peter 1:19 of Christ's blood): koitē amiantos: undefiled bed — koitē: the marriage bed as a metonymy for the sexual relationship; amiantos: unstained, same word as 7:26 for Christ as unstained. Pornous kai moichous krinei ho theos: God judges the sexually immoral and the adulterous — present tense: the divine judgment is ongoing and certain; pornos (one who practices sexual immorality) and moichos (adulterer, one who violates the marriage covenant) cover both pre-marital and extra-marital violations.
5: Aphilēargyros ho tropos; arkoumenoi tois parousin; autos gar eirēken, Ou mē se anō oud' ou mē se egkataleipō — "keep your life free from love of money, and be content with what you have, for he has said: Never will I leave you; never will I forsake you" (aphilēargyros ho tropos: let your manner of life be free from love of silver/money — a-privative + philos + argyros: the compound adjective; tropos: manner of life, disposition): Ou mē se anō oud' ou mē se egkataleipō: the double negative ou mē used twice, the strongest possible Greek negation, translating a Hebrew emphatic promise (Joshua 1:5; Deuteronomy 31:6, 8): the divine promise of non-abandonment is given as the logical ground for the contentment-command — one who holds the divine promise of presence has no rational basis for anxious acquisitiveness.
6: hōste tharroūntas hēmas legein, Kyrios emoi boēthos, [kai] ou phobēthēsomai; ti poiēsei moi anthrōpos — "so we say with confidence: The Lord is my helper; I will not be afraid. What can anyone do to me?" (Psalms 118:6 LXX): tharroūntas: having confidence, being bold — present active participle of tharreō: to be bold, to take courage; the confidence produces the declaration. Boēthos: helper, one who runs to the cry (cf. Hebrews 2:18: boēthēsai) — the divine title echoes the high-priestly help of the earlier chapters. Ti poiēsei moi anthrōpos: what shall a human being do to me? — anthrōpos here is the mortal human in contrast to the divine Helper; the rhetorical question assumes the answer: nothing that matters ultimately.
9: didachais poikilais kai xenais mē parapheresthe; kalon gar chariti bebaiouesthai tēn kardian, ou brōmasin, en hois ouk ōphelēthēsan hoi peripatountes — "do not be carried away by all kinds of strange teachings. It is good for the heart to be strengthened by grace, not by food regulations, which are of no benefit to those who observe them" (didachais poikilais kai xenais: various and foreign teachings — poikilos: many-colored, varied, diverse; xenos: foreign, strange, belonging to another tradition; the combination signals novelty and foreignness): parapheresthe: do not be carried away/swept along — the nautical image recurs (cf. Hebrews 2:1, pararrōmen); chariti: by grace — the contrast between charis and brōmata (foods) is the theological point: what stabilizes the heart is grace (the new covenant gift), not the observance of dietary regulations whose ritual significance has been superseded.
11: hōn gar eisipheretai zōōn to haima peri hamartias eis ta hagia dia tou archiereōs, toutōn ta sōmata katakaietai exō tēs parembolas — "for the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the Most Holy Place by the high priest as a sin offering are burned outside the camp" (Leviticus 16:27): exō tēs parembolas: outside the camp — the Levitical sin-offering whose blood entered the sanctuary had its carcass burned outside the camp (not consumed); the blood went in, the body went out. Parembola: camp — the word for the Israelite wilderness camp; Hebrews uses it deliberately to frame the typology in Mosaic terms before applying it to Christ's crucifixion outside Jerusalem's gate (v12).
13: toinun exerchōmetha pros auton exō tēs parembolas, ton oneidismon autou pherontes — "let us, then, go to him outside the camp, bearing the disgrace he bore" (toinun exerchōmetha: therefore let us go out — the inferential particle toinun introduces the application drawn from the typology; exerchōmetha: hortatory subjunctive: the author includes himself in the call): exō tēs parembolas (outside the camp): the spatial movement required of the community — going out from the security of the religious establishment that crucified Christ and joining him in the social margins. Ton oneidismon autou pherontes: bearing his disgrace/reproach — the same oneidismos that Moses bore (Hebrews 11:26); the reproach is not incidental to the journey but its content.
14: ou gar echomen hōde menousan polin, alla tēn mellousan epizētoumen — "for here we do not have an enduring city, but we are looking for the city that is to come" (menousan polin: an abiding/remaining city — menousa: present participle of menō: the city that remains, the permanent city): the city-language binds together the entire letter's climax: the patriarchs looked for a city (Hebrews 11:10, 16), Mount Zion is the heavenly Jerusalem (Hebrews 12:22), and the community has no permanent earthly city. Tēn mellousan: the coming one — the same mellō root as the coming age, the coming good things; the community's citizenship is in the city that is still arriving.
16: tēs de eupoiias kai koinōnias mē epilanthanethe; toiautais gar thysiais euaresteitai ho theos — "and do not forget to do good and to share with others, for with such sacrifices God is pleased" (eupoiia: doing good, beneficence — eu + poieō: the compound for well-doing; a NT hapax): koinōnias: sharing, fellowship — the economic and social solidarity of the community. Toiautais thysiais: with such sacrifices — the vocabulary of sacrifice is applied to concrete acts of generosity; the sacrificial system has not simply ended but been transformed: the offerings God receives now are praise (v15) and beneficence (v16). The two new-covenant sacrifices match the old covenant's two dimensions: the upward offering to God (praise) and the horizontal sharing with others.
17: Peithesthe tois hēgoumenois hymōn kai hypeikete; autoi gar agrypnousin hyper tōn psychōn hymōn hōs logon apodōsontes — "have confidence in your leaders and submit to their authority, because they keep watch over you as those who must give an account" (agrypnousin: they keep watch, lie awake for — present tense: the ongoing watchfulness; agrypneō: to be sleepless, to lie awake watching; the image of a shepherd who cannot sleep while the flock is in danger): hōs logon apodōsontes: as those about to give an account — apodidōmi: to give back, to render; the account is rendered to God, not to the community. The leaders' accountability to God is both the ground of their authority and the limit of it. Hina meta charas touto poiōsin kai mē stenazontes: so that they do this with joy and not with groaning.
18: Proseuchesthe peri hēmōn; peithometha gar hoti kalēn syneidēsin echomen — "pray for us. We are sure that we have a clear conscience and desire to live honorably in every way" (kalēn syneidēsin: a beautiful/good conscience — kalos: good in the qualitative sense of beauty and excellence; the syneidēsis [conscience] that the sprinkled blood of Christ has cleansed [Hebrews 9:14] is now described as kalē): en pasin kalōs thelontes anastraphēnai: desiring to conduct ourselves well in all things — anastraphēnai: to conduct oneself, to live; the conscience claim is accompanied by the desire for corresponding conduct. The prayer-request grounds the author's pastoral authority in personal integrity.
19: perissoteros de parakalō touto poiēsai, hina tachion apokatastathō hymin — "I particularly urge you to pray so that I may be restored to you soon" (perissoteros parakalō: I urge more abundantly/especially — the superlative adverb intensifying the urgency): apokatastathō: be restored — aorist passive subjunctive of apokathistēmi: to restore fully to the original position; the same verb for eschatological restoration. Tachion: more quickly, sooner — comparative of tachus: the author's desire is for an expedited reunion. The personal note grounds the theological treatise in an actual relationship between author and community.
21: katartisai hymas en panti agathō eis to poiēsai to thelēma autou, poiōn en hēmin to euareston enōpion autou dia Iēsou Christou — "equip you with everything good for doing his will, and may he work in us what is pleasing to him, through Jesus Christ" (katartisai: equip, restore, fit out — the same verb as Hebrews 10:5 [body you prepared/fitted] and Ephesians 4:12 [equipping of the saints]): to euareston: what is pleasing, the pleasing thing — the verbal noun captures the quality of divine approval; poiōn en hēmin: working in us — God is the agent of both the equipping and the performing; the prayer acknowledges that the doing of God's will is itself a divine work within the community. Dia Iēsou Christou: through Jesus Christ — the mediatorial channel of all divine working.
22: Parakalō de hymas, adelphoi, anechesthe tou logou tēs paraklēseōs, kai gar dia bracheōn epestila hymin — "brothers and sisters, I urge you to bear with my word of exhortation, for in fact I have written to you quite briefly" (logou tēs paraklēseōs: word of exhortation/encouragement — paraklēsis: the calling-alongside to encourage, strengthen, or exhort; a self-identification of the letter's genre): dia bracheōn epestila: I wrote through brief/short means — dia bracheōn: briefly, concisely; the author is aware that what the community might find difficult to receive has been presented as briefly as possible. The letter is long by NT standards; "briefly" is relative to the depth of the material covered.
23: ginōskete ton adelphon Timotheon apolelumenon, meth' hou, ean tachion erchētai, opsōmai hymas — "I want you to know that our brother Timothy has been released. If he arrives soon, I will come with him to see you" (apolelumenon: having been released — perfect passive participle of apoluō: to release, set free; the same word for releasing from prison or for the Levitical dismissal): the mention of Timothy (ton adelphon Timotheon: the brother Timothy) and his release from some form of custody provides the one unambiguous personal identification in the letter. The conditional ean tachion erchētai (if he arrives soon enough) ties the author's intended visit to Timothy's freedom of travel.
24: Aspasasthe pantas tous hēgoumenous hymōn kai pantas tous hagious; aspazontai hymas hoi apo tēs Italias — "greet all your leaders and all the Lord's people. Those from Italy send you their greetings" (Aspasasthe: greet — the standard epistolary closing formula): pantas... pantas (all... all): the double pantas emphasizes comprehensiveness — no leader and no holy person is excluded from the greeting. Hoi apo tēs Italias: those from Italy — a phrase that can mean either those currently in Italy (writing to the recipients) or Italian expatriates who are with the author and send greetings back home; the latter reading supports the hypothesis of Roman recipients.
25: Hē charis meta pantōn hymōn — "grace be with you all" (Hē charis: the grace — with the article: a specific grace, the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ): the minimal epistolary benediction. Meta pantōn hymōn: with all of you — the pantōn echoes the universal scope of the greeting in v24; the closing grace encompasses the entire community. Charis as the closing word gathers the theological heart of the letter: the grace mediated by the eternal high priest who entered once for all into the heavenly sanctuary is the final word to the community that is called to draw near with confidence.
